EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the afternoon and evening of Wednesday, July 14.  Some of the storms produced damaging winds and numerous tornadoes.  Brief torrential rainfall was also observed with the stronger storm cells.  The areas that were hit hardest were in Buchanan, Delaware, Benton, Linn, and Jones counties in IA, where a total of 14 tornadoes occurred.  7 of the tornadoes had no visible damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief touchdown in a field with no visible damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado developed southeast of Monticello and tracked across rural areas east of Monticello along an intermittent 8 mile path through farmland and wooded areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm produced 1 inch hail in Fremont County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 1 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few supercell thunderstorms developed ahead of a warm front advancing into the forecast area, where they produced gusty winds, heavy rain, and hail up to 3 inches in diameter. Storms initially developed as more discrete supercells early in the evening, with the storm that went through Burlington being one of the more intense storms. Rainfall amounts reached 2 inches or more in some areas, as the anomalously moist environment favored excessive rainfall that lead to flash flooding. The La Moine River near Colmar also raised to Minor Flood stage due to the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large outflow boundary from a late night convective complex across northeast South Dakota and west central MN pushed southward, and interacted with a strong low-level jet. With steep mid-level lapse rates present, convective initiation resulted in intense updrafts along the boundary which produced large hail.

Heat and humidity led to strong instability by afternoon, as a weak frontal boundary settled into southeast South Dakota. Strong instability but weak deep-layer shear resulted in brief, intense and generally outflow-dominant storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree limbs up to six inches in diameter were broken by wind gusts.

With a move towards more near-normal temperatures and near-normal to above-normal precipitation for the month of July, drought status reductions from D2 - Severe Drought to D1 - Moderate Drought or D0 - Abnormally Dry were experienced across portions of central and western Iowa. While streamflow also remained below normal, improvement was experienced in many of the area streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A return to near-normal temperatures and near to above-normal precipitation allowed areas of central and western Iowa to see drought reductions from D2 - Severe Drought to D1 - Moderate Drought or D0 - Abnormally Dry conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of central and western Iowa found themselves in a transition area of precipitation with areas to the north and east remaining below normal and areas to the south and west transitioning to near and above-normal amounts. Coupled with a return to near-normal temperatures, drought conditions receded within these counties by mid-July (July 13th Drought Monitor) and remained unchanged through the end of the month. Streamflow values largely remained well below normal in these areas as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A return to near-normal temperatures and lying within a transition zone of precipitation, ranging from continued below normal to the north and east to near and above-normal to the south and west, allowed some D2 - Severe Drought conditions to improve but not be eliminated throughout the county. D2 coverage was largely reduced by the July 13th issuance of the Drought Monitor, and remained stable through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were largely near-normal in July, continued below normal precipitation largely contributed to the general expansion of Severe Drought in areas of northeast Iowa. Within the month, drought conditions wavered between D1 and D2, in part thanks to timely, albeit below normal, rainfall. Streamflow conditions remained well below normal for the time of the year as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near normal temperatures, but below normal precipitation drove the general expansion of Severe Drought during July. Timely bits of rainfall allowed some wavering between D1 and D2 drought within the month though.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracked across the Upper Midwest from December 10th into the 11th. The storm brought snow and strong winds to parts of northeast Iowa. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches were common across Mitchell and Howard Counties. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred as gusts of 30 to 40 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th. The highest reported snowfall total was 5.6 inches in St Ansgar (Mitchell County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 and 5 inches of snow was estimated to have fallen across the northwest half of Howard County. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th as north winds gusted into the 30 to 40 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed behind a southward sagging frontal boundary.  With around 1000 J/kg MLCAPE and 40-45 knots of effective shear, a brief supercellular structure evolved. Even with hail size somewhat limited by available instability, substantial damage to total destruction of crops occurred due to the large quantity of wind-driven hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind-driven hail produced considerable to total crop damage. Damage began around 4 miles southeast of Le Mars, and followed a nearly continuous 1 to 3 mile wide path for 17 miles to the southeast, crossing into Cherokee and Woodbury County around 6 miles east southeast of Kingsley.  Hail varied from dime to quarter size along the path, with quarter size hail 3 miles north northeast of Kingsley at 1734 CST and 5 miles east northeast of Kingsley at 1738 CST.  Winds were estimated as high as 60 to 65 mph. Damage was done to the siding of a house 1.5 miles east of O Leary at 1720 CST.  The worst crop damage occurred from 3 miles north to 4 miles east of Kingsley, where hail was still 3 inches deep on roadways an hour after the storm.  The path was determined from surface reports and Sentinel satellite data. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois on the afternoon of Tuesday, August 10 2021. Storms produced mainly damaging winds in an environment with plentiful instability, with a gust estimated at 70 MPH occurring in Linn County. Damage to trees was reported in and around the Cedar Rapids metro east to Freeport IL. Some reports of hail were received as well, but were mainly at quarter-sized or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported blown down on northeast side of Marion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought strong (both thunderstorm, and non-thunderstorm) winds, tornadoes, dust, snow, and record temperatures into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. The event was determined to be a derecho meeting the criteria of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow EF-2 tornado began producing damage near Mahogany Road and 290th St and tracked north northeast into the southeast edge of Neola and then caused intermittent damage near Railroad Highway North of Neola. The most significant damage was to a home along 290th street where the roof was removed and walls blown out. A nearby garage was moved off of its foundation and approximately 10 mature trees were all uprooted behind the home with substantial damage to hardwood trees in the front yard. The narrow nature of this tornado was evident in the damage path as nearby barns on the east side of the track had low-end damage, possibly owing to the significant forward speed of the tornado. The tornado was seen briefly by the resident before seeking shelter. A business on the southeast side of Neola also had substantial damage to large metal buildings, at least two of which were destroyed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short-lived tornado developed on a farm where it quickly destroyed a metal building structure, flipping two trucks that were parked inside the building. The roof was flipped off of the structure and debris was distributed to the northeast. The tornado then impacted and snapped several wood power poles but did not cause any additional significant or highly focused damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few supercell thunderstorms developed ahead of a warm front advancing into the forecast area, where they produced gusty winds, heavy rain, and hail up to 3 inches in diameter. Storms initially developed as more discrete supercells early in the evening, with the storm that went through Burlington being one of the more intense storms. Rainfall amounts reached 2 inches or more in some areas, as the anomalously moist environment favored excessive rainfall that lead to flash flooding. The La Moine River near Colmar also raised to Minor Flood stage due to the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel shortwave moved through the area producing storms with severe criteria hail and wind gusts. In Council Bluffs, 2 to 3 inches of rain fell over about an hour which led to a flash flood warning being issued. Flash flooding washed through basement apartments, swept up cars, and heaved pavement in low-lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 2-4 feet of water over roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged cold snap that ensnared much of the country intensified to it's most extreme levels across Iowa February 13 through 16 a reinforcing shot of extreme cold air out of Canada settled in. Daytime air temperatures remained in the single digits and teens below zero with overnight lows in the 20s below zero. Northerly winds resulted in wind chills in the -30 to -40s area wide. Temperatures remained bitterly cold until weak southerly flow allowed temperatures to return to more climatologically typical values.

A western upper level trough underwent lee cyclogenesis over the central Rockies and into the central Plains. Strong moisture transport out of the Gulf region and from the pacific northwest into the system with strong lift within the dendritic growth zone made for periods of moderate to heavy snow with rates exceeding an inch per hour. Precipitation began with a very narrow window of drizzle before transitioning over to snow behind the cold front. Strong winds behind the front gusted over 40 mph behind the front, resulting in blizzard or near blizzard conditions across much of northern and into central Iowa. The high shear also allowed for the development of horizontal convective rolls over northern Iowa, causing rapid reductions in visibility within the rolls. A number of car accidents occurred due to reduced visibility and slick road conditions including two multi car pile ups. A 30 car pile up in Jasper county and a 10 car pile up in Polk county. Non-traditional sources such as impacts, webcams, and spotter reports, were evaluated to classify this event as a Blizzard over rural areas.

A 500 mb trough moved across the Rocky Mountains with attendant surface low deepening across the central plains and lifting northeast into Iowa. Moisture transport increased out of the Gulf region and into the area combined with strong isentropic lift focused within the dendritic growth zone. This resulted in snow rates over an inch per hours across parts of western and central Iowa, impacting travel across the area.

Arctic high pressure built across northern and northeast portions of the state following a light snow event that fell on top of an already fairly deep snowpack across the state. Fresh snow and clear skies under the high allowed temperatures to plummet into the single digits and teens below zero. Combined with northerly winds, wind chills in the early morning hours dropped to -30 or colder at many locations.

Non-traditional sources such as impacts, webcams, and spotter reports, were evaluated to classify this event as a Blizzard over rural areas.

Arctic high pressure built across northern and northeast portions of the state following a light snow event that fell on top of an already fairly deep snowpack across the state. Fresh snow and clear skies under the high allowed temperatures to plummet into the single digits and teens below zero. Combined with northerly winds, wind chills in the early morning hours dropped to -30 or colder at many locations.

Strong moisture transport out of the Gulf region and from the pacific northwest into the system with strong lift within the dendritic growth zone made for periods of moderate to heavy snow with rates exceeding an inch per hour. Precipitation began with a very narrow window of drizzle before transitioning over to snow behind the cold front. Strong winds behind the front gusted over 40 mph behind the front, resulting in blizzard or near blizzard conditions across much of northern and into central Iowa. The high shear also allowed for the development of horizontal convective rolls over northern Iowa, causing rapid reductions in visibility within the rolls. A number of car accidents occurred due to reduced visibility and slick road conditions including two multi car pile ups. A 30 car pile up in Jasper county and a 10 car pile up in Polk county. Non-traditional sources such as impacts, webcams, and spotter reports, were evaluated to classify this event as a Blizzard over rural areas.

A prolonged cold snap that ensnared much of the country intensified to it's most extreme levels across Iowa February 13 through 16 a reinforcing shot of extreme cold air out of Canada settled in. Daytime air temperatures remained in the single digits and teens below zero with overnight lows in the 20s below zero. Northerly winds resulted in wind chills in the -30 to -40s area wide. Temperatures remained bitterly cold until weak southerly flow allowed temperatures to return to more climatologically typical values.

A line of storms that developed over western Nebraska and South Dakota moved into a highly unstable airmass with 2500-3000 J/kg MLCAPE leading to widespread wind damage across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. According to the Omaha Public Power District, nearly 200,000 customers were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa mesonet site measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of northeast Iowa suffered through blizzard conditions during the afternoon of February 4th. A storm system produced 3 to 5 inches of snow along with winds of 35 to 45 mph that reduced the visibility to under a quarter mile for a few hours and drifted roads shut with travel not advised across the region for much of the afternoon. The automated weather observing equipment at both the Charles City (Floyd County) and Oelwein (Fayette County) airports reported 4 to 5 hours when the visibility was reduced to a quarter mile or less by falling and blowing snow. Most schools canceled classes for the day because of the storm. The highest reported snowfall amount was 5 inches around New Hampton (Chickasaw County) and the highest recorded wind gust was 44 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer observers reported that around 4 inches of snow fell across Clayton County. Roads across the county became snow covered and travel was not advised at times during the afternoon. Several schools across the county canceled classes for the day. The highest reported snowfall amount was 4.1 inches southwest of Elkader.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another shot of arctic air settled in over northeast Iowa and produced dangerously cold wind chills of 35 to 40 below zero from the early morning of February 14th into the morning of the 15th. The wind chills occurred with air temperatures in the single digits and teens below zero along with winds of 10 to 15 mph. The coldest recorded wind chills were 40 below zero on the morning of the 14th at mesonet stations near New Hampton (Chickasaw County) and near Elma (Howard County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously cold wind chills of 35 to 40 below zero occurred from the early morning of February 14th into the morning of the 15th. These wind chills occurred with temperatures in the single digits and teens below zero and winds of 10 to 15 mph. The coldest recorded wind chill was 40 below zero at a mesonet station near Elma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold air with temperatures from 10 to 20 below zero settled in over northeast Iowa during the early morning hours of February 7th. Combined with northwest winds around 10 mph, dangerously cold winds chills of 35 to 45 below zero occurred. The coldest recorded wind chill was -43 by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein (Fayette County) airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of air temperatures in the teens below zero and northwest winds around 10 mph produced dangerously cold wind chills between 35 and 40 below zero. The coldest recorded wind chill was 38 below zero by a mesonet station northeast of New Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive arctic plunge into the Northern Plains brought almost a week of dangerous and life threatening wind chills to the region. The worst conditions were recorded between February 13 and February 16 when wind chills occasionally dropped as low as 35 below to 55 below zero. Numerous record low maximum and minimum temperatures were also established. The areal extent of the extreme cold in the central portions of the U.S. led to stress on the power grid, which necessitated several short-term rolling power outages impacting more than 10,000 customers during the peak of the cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During several periods over three days with wind chill below -35, values reached a minimum of -42 at Ida Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving upper level wave amplified while sliding eastward across the Central Plains.  Snowfall developed quickly ahead of this feature, with broad snowfall in and near northwest Iowa collapsing into a narrow band due to the presence of mid-level instability above a lower-level frontogenetic zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reduced visibility to one-quarter to one-half mile at times between 0700-1200CST and produced treacherous travel conditions. Accumulation reached 8.0 inches 4 miles north northeast of Hornick, 6.5 inches 1 mile east of Bronson and 6.0 inches at Sergeant Bluff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front pushed into an environment of large MLCAPE (2000-3000 J/kg) and high DCAPE (1000 J/kg), scattered elevated thunderstorms developed on the nose of a 40 knot low-level jet across southwest Minnesota. These storms dropped south southeast through northwest Iowa during the evening producing periodic large hail and some damaging wind gusts as the low-level jet veered ahead of the advancing boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An old refrigerator was displaced across the deck of a house by thunderstorm wind estimated from 60 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving arctic cold front brought dangerous conditions to portions of the area. Rain, mixed with freezing rain and sleet moved into eastern Iowa during the early morning, then as a cold front moved in from the west during the mid morning, the rain switched to snow. Strong winds and falling temperatures behind the front caused wet roads to quickly freeze.  Snow fell for several hours after the change over and became heavy at times, with widespread 2 to 6 inches of snowfall. Winds behind the front were 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 45 mph during the afternoon and evening causing blowing snow in open areas. Whiteout conditions were seen in open areas, and some areas experienced periods of near blizzard conditions, especially across east central and  northeast Iowa. Significant drifting snow continued into Feb. 5.  Wind chills dropped to 5 below to 15 below zero late Thursday night and Friday morning along and north of I-80.||Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, and road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain and wintry mix started the day, leading to snow (heavy at times), where between 2 and 5 inches of snow fell throughout Clinton County. Strong winds were also noted in the afternoon, with a peak gust at 45 mph reported at the Clinton Airport AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front was accompanied by a narrow band of moderate to heavy snowfall, as well as wind gusting to 30 to 45 mph.  Most areas saw a brief period of rain, freezing rain or sleet before changing to snow, which was accompanied by a temperature fall of 10 to 20 degrees over a six hour period.  Contraction of the band occurred due to strong elevated instability, with isolated thunder occurring in southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota.  While most areas received around an inch or two of snowfall, the band peaked in intensity around the Sioux Falls area with up to three to five inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was briefly heavy and totaled 2.0 inches at Sioux City. Roads became hazardous, and frequent wind gusts as high as 49 mph at Sioux City produced several hours of blowing snow with visibility as low as one-half mile.

Roads became hazardous, and frequent wind gusts as high as 31 mph at Cherokee produced several hours of blowing snow with visibility as low as one-half to one mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of northeast Iowa suffered through blizzard conditions during the afternoon of February 4th. A storm system produced 3 to 5 inches of snow along with winds of 35 to 45 mph that reduced the visibility to under a quarter mile for a few hours and drifted roads shut with travel not advised across the region for much of the afternoon. The automated weather observing equipment at both the Charles City (Floyd County) and Oelwein (Fayette County) airports reported 4 to 5 hours when the visibility was reduced to a quarter mile or less by falling and blowing snow. Most schools canceled classes for the day because of the storm. The highest reported snowfall amount was 5 inches around New Hampton (Chickasaw County) and the highest recorded wind gust was 44 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm dropped 3 to 4 inches of snow across Floyd County. A period of blizzard conditions occurred in the afternoon when winds gusted to between 35 and 40 mph and the combination of falling and blowing snow reduced the visibility to a quarter mile or less. Travel was not advised as roads were completely snow covered and some were closed by drifts. Most schools across the county canceled classes for the day. The highest reported snowfall total was 4 inches near Nora Springs and the highest recorded wind gust was 38 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Charles City airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive arctic plunge into the Northern Plains brought almost a week of dangerous and life threatening wind chills to the region. The worst conditions were recorded between February 13 and February 16 when wind chills occasionally dropped as low as 35 below to 55 below zero. Numerous record low maximum and minimum temperatures were also established. The areal extent of the extreme cold in the central portions of the U.S. led to stress on the power grid, which necessitated several short-term rolling power outages impacting more than 10,000 customers during the peak of the cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During several periods over three days with wind chill below -35, values reached a minimum of -44 at Beaver Creek and -42 at Sheldon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving upper level wave amplified while sliding eastward across the Central Plains.  Snowfall developed quickly ahead of this feature, with broad snowfall in and near northwest Iowa collapsing into a narrow band due to the presence of mid-level instability above a lower-level frontogenetic zone.

Snow fell for several hours after the change over and became heavy at times, with widespread 2 to 6 inches of snowfall. Winds behind the front were 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 45 mph during the afternoon and evening causing blowing snow in open areas. Whiteout conditions were seen in open areas, and some areas experienced periods of near blizzard conditions, especially across east central and  northeast Iowa. Significant drifting snow continued into Feb. 5.  Wind chills dropped to 5 below to 15 below zero late Thursday night and Friday morning along and north of I-80.||Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, and road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain and wintry mix started the day, leading to snow (heavy at times), where between 1 and 4 inches of snow fell throughout Henry County. Strong winds were also noted in the afternoon, with a peak gusts between 25 to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of northeast Iowa suffered through blizzard conditions during the afternoon of February 4th. A storm system produced 3 to 5 inches of snow along with winds of 35 to 45 mph that reduced the visibility to under a quarter mile for a few hours and drifted roads shut with travel not advised across the region for much of the afternoon. The automated weather observing equipment at both the Charles City (Floyd County) and Oelwein (Fayette County) airports reported 4 to 5 hours when the visibility was reduced to a quarter mile or less by falling and blowing snow. Most schools canceled classes for the day because of the storm. The highest reported snowfall amount was 5 inches around New Hampton (Chickasaw County) and the highest recorded wind gust was 44 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm dropped around 4 inches of snow across Howard County. Roads across the county became snow covered and travel was not advised at times during the afternoon. Schools across the county canceled classes for the day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm dropped around 3 inches of snow across Fayette County. A period of blizzard conditions occurred in the afternoon when winds gusted to between 35 and 45 mph and the combination of falling and blowing snow reduced the visibility to a quarter mile or less. Travel was not advised as roads were completely snow covered and some were closed by drifts. Schools across the county canceled classes for the day. The highest reported snowfall total was 3 inches in Fayette and the highest recorded wind gust was 44 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent below normal precipitation since February 2021 allowed severe drought conditions to develop across most of northeast Iowa. These conditions continued into July 2021 for Mitchell, Howard, Winneshiek, Floyd, Chickasaw and Fayette Counties. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches during the last part of June allowed soil moisture conditions to improve and brought an end to the severe drought across Allamakee and Clayton Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent below normal precipitation since February 2021 allowed severe drought conditions to develop across Allamakee County. Rainfall up to 2 inches during the last part of June allowed soil moisture conditions to improve and brought an end to the severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary thunderstorm developed in Linn County, near Marion, Iowa. This thunderstorm formed in a favorable environment for heavy rain, which it led to nearly 6 inches or more in some areas within a few hours. This led to rapid flash flooding within neighborhoods, as Indian Creek came over and out of its banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Evening rainfall total of 1.92 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the afternoon of September 20th as a cold front pushed through the region. Some of these storms produced strong, damaging winds. Wind gusts were estimated to be around 65 mph in Charles City (Floyd County) where some tree branches were blown down. Trees were also blown down in Bassett (Chickasaw County) and a 60 mph gust occurred near Guttenberg (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be 60 to 65 mph occurred in Charles City. Some tree branches, up to 6 inches in diameter, were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong thunderstorms and heavy rain moved through Iowa on the 24th, which led to flash flooding for some areas. The areas seeing the largest impact were in Jefferson and Van Buren Counties in Iowa. The environment in which these storms were in not only favored thunderstorms, but heavy rain, as moisture indices were near record level. Very efficient rain rates were seen, where 2 to 3 inches per hour were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight rainfall total of 2.61 inches since 930 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the 90s and high moisture in the low levels led to diurnally driven thunderstorms on Friday, June 11th. Weak upper level support kept these storms relatively isolated and short-lived. Another focus area for convection was along a remnant outflow boundary from an MCS that morning, which led to some further storm development later in the day. One storm reached severe limits, which was seen in Burlington, Iowa. This storm resulted in strong downburst winds over 70 mph, that lead to multiple damaged trees and some buildings damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of a church was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the afternoon and evening of Wednesday, July 14. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow-moving area of low pressure wandered from Colorado to eastern South Dakota over a two-day period. Precipitation circulated back into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, which started as a period of rainfall prior to evening on March 14. Rain mixed and changed to snow during the late evening and early overnight. Snow became heavy at times during overnight, with snowfall rates up to 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was heavy at times between 0000CST and 0400CST on March 15, resulting in very hazardous travel conditions. A total of 6.0 inches at Rock Rapids was recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed behind a southward sagging frontal boundary.  With around 1000 J/kg MLCAPE and 40-45 knots of effective shear, a brief supercellular structure evolved. Even with hail size somewhat limited by available instability, substantial damage to total destruction of crops occurred due to the large quantity of wind-driven hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind-driven hail damage path continued out of Plymouth and Cherokee Counties into Woodbury County, where the continuous path ended around 2 miles east southeast of Pierson. The largest hail reported along the path was observed near Pierson with golf ball size hail at 1748 CST.  Half dollar size hail was reported at 1755 CST 3.5 miles north northwest of Correctionville. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep western trough underwent lee-cyclogenesis across the southern Rockies with the low center tracking northeast across the southern Plains and into the midwest. Central and southern Iowa remained well within the deformation zone of the cyclone, allowing for the heaviest snow to fall in a band from southwest Iowa through central Iowa with a tight accumulation gradient across northern Iowa.  Forcing was particularly strong with the system, with lift within the dendritic growth zone exceeding -20 ubar/s for large portions of the state. Many areas saw snow rates exceed 1 inch per hour.

A strong storm system moved into the Midwest on the afternoon of January 30, 2021. Initially, precipitation began as rain as a layer of warm air was in place aloft. Over time, however, temperatures began to cool at the surface and aloft due to the falling rain, and precipitation changed to snow during the evening as a result. Some freezing rain was even measured as the transition occurred, and was most noticeable in the Cedar Rapids metro where photos showed accumulation on power lines.||Snow was widespread across the area during the morning of January 31, 2021, and all of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and far northeast Missouri saw some form of accumulating snow. Totals were highest in parts of east central Iowa and in north central Illinois, where locally heavy banding at times on the morning of January 31 produced higher snowfall rates. Overall, totals ranged from around 4 to 6 inches, with some locally higher amounts of 7 to 8 inches reported in Buchanan and Benton counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer 2 miles north of Muscatine reported 3.8 inches of snow along with a glaze of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weaker winter storm moved into the area and added more ice and snow to the snow pack received the other day. This system moved in from the south and brought along a strong pull of warm air aloft. This led to a lot more ice accumulation than was expected prior to the event. While the storm was supposed to lead to mostly snow for the northern half of our forecast area, those areas received mainly freezing rain and sleet through most of the afternoon. This led to widespread ice accumulations of 0.10-0.25. In the southwest part of our forecast area, more ice accumulation was noted, with many locations reporting around 0.25 or even more. Along with the accumulating ice, some downed tree limbs and power outages were a result.||Mid afternoon, the freezing rain began to switch to snow from a line around Iowa City, IA to Freeport, IL. A relatively strong band formed in this location and dropped around 4 of snow, especially in the Iowa City area.  There were widespread reports of 2.3 inches of snow and sleet accumulation across eastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Official NWS observations at the Davenport Municipal Airport were 0.7 of snow and sleet. A public report of 2.5 inches of snow and sleet 2 miles west southwest of Davenport. A trained spotter one mile west of Bettendorf estimated a quarter of an inch of freezing rain. An NWS employee reported a quarter of an inch of ice 2 miles east northeast of Davenport.

There were widespread reports of 2.3 inches of snow and sleet accumulation across eastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from 2.0 inches 1 mile west of Elmira to .5 inches 1 mile east of Iowa City. 4.0 inches was also reported 2 miles south southeast of Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of mid-level frontogenesis set up near the Missouri River and eastward across northwest Iowa during the afternoon and evening of January 25, bringing a swath of moderate to briefly heavy snowfall. Dry air to the north resulted in a tight gradient to amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 7.0 inches 4 miles north northeast of Hornick and 5.7 inches in Sioux City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northwesterly jet streak digging through the central Rockies produced intense and deep cyclogenesis across Iowa on January 14 and 15. Initial precipitation swept through the area as rain, but a surge of cold air into the slowing cyclone wrapped snowfall back into northeast Nebraska as winds increased to 40 to 60 mph, resulting in periods of blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As snow developed and began to accumulate during the evening on January 14, the increase in winds to a sustained 30 to 40 mph, with a few gusts as high as 51 mph at an Iowa Department of Transportation site at Alton and 44 mph near Maurice (SXK), resulted in widespread near white-out conditions. Shortly after snowfall began, a vehicle lost control and collided with another traveling in the opposite direction west of Alton, resulting in an indirect fatality to one driver and an indirect injury to the other. Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches at Sioux Center and 2.5 inches at Orange City.

Over time, however, temperatures began to cool at the surface and aloft due to the falling rain, and precipitation changed to snow during the evening as a result. Some freezing rain was even measured as the transition occurred, and was most noticeable in the Cedar Rapids metro where photos showed accumulation on power lines.||Snow was widespread across the area during the morning of January 31, 2021, and all of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and far northeast Missouri saw some form of accumulating snow. Totals were highest in parts of east central Iowa and in north central Illinois, where locally heavy banding at times on the morning of January 31 produced higher snowfall rates. Overall, totals ranged from around 4 to 6 inches, with some locally higher amounts of 7 to 8 inches reported in Buchanan and Benton counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from 3.5 inches from a trained spotter in Clearance to 4.6 inches in Lowden from the COOP observer. A trained spotter in Durant reported 0.5 inches of freezing rain under the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread snowfall amounts of 3 to 8 inches to much of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and far northeast Missouri from the afternoon of January 25th into the afternoon of January 26th. The heaviest snow amounts were recorded in central Iowa, where values were reported around 10 to 14 inches.  In addition, a light ice glaze was also seen in parts of extreme southeast Iowa, northeast Missouri, and west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official NWS observation at Davenport Municipal Airport was 7.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep western trough underwent lee-cyclogenesis across the southern Rockies with the low center tracking northeast across the southern Plains and into the midwest. Central and southern Iowa remained well within the deformation zone of the cyclone, allowing for the heaviest snow to fall in a band from southwest Iowa through central Iowa with a tight accumulation gradient across northern Iowa.  Forcing was particularly strong with the system, with lift within the dendritic growth zone exceeding -20 ubar/s for large portions of the state. Many areas saw snow rates exceed 1 inch per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northwesterly jet streak digging through the central Rockies produced intense and deep cyclogenesis across Iowa on January 14 and 15. Initial precipitation swept through the area as rain, but a surge of cold air into the slowing cyclone wrapped snowfall back into northeast Nebraska as winds increased to 40 to 60 mph, resulting in periods of blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As snow developed and began to accumulate during the evening on January 14, the increase in winds to a sustained 30 to 40 mph, with a few gusts as high as 52 mph at Storm Lake (SLB) and 49 mph at an Iowa Dept. of Transportation site 2 miles east of Truesdale, resulted in widespread near white-out conditions. Snowfall totaled 4.7 inches at Storm Lake and 3.6 inches at Sioux Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weaker winter storm moved into the area and added more ice and snow to the snow pack received the other day. This system moved in from the south and brought along a strong pull of warm air aloft. This led to a lot more ice accumulation than was expected prior to the event. While the storm was supposed to lead to mostly snow for the northern half of our forecast area, those areas received mainly freezing rain and sleet through most of the afternoon. This led to widespread ice accumulations of 0.10-0.25. In the southwest part of our forecast area, more ice accumulation was noted, with many locations reporting around 0.25 or even more. Along with the accumulating ice, some downed tree limbs and power outages were a result.||Mid afternoon, the freezing rain began to switch to snow from a line around Iowa City, IA to Freeport, IL. A relatively strong band formed in this location and dropped around 4 of snow, especially in the Iowa City area.  There were widespread reports of 2.3 inches of snow and sleet accumulation across eastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer reported 0.8 inches of snow and sleet along with a glaze of ice. There were two public reports of 0.25 to 0.30 inches of freezing rain near Muscatine as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger 500 mb closed low moved across the northern plains and into the midwest, where it stalled and moved quite slowly across the state. The deformation zone on the northwest side of the system dropped several inches of snow across the area, however strong winds producing blowing snow was the bigger hazard. With a very tight pressure gradient in place, winds gusted between 40 to 50 mph across the area, which combined with falling snow to create significantly reduced visibility across much of western Iowa on the morning of January 15th.

A strong northwesterly jet streak digging through the central Rockies produced intense and deep cyclogenesis across Iowa on January 14 and 15. Initial precipitation swept through the area as rain, but a surge of cold air into the slowing cyclone wrapped snowfall back into northeast Nebraska as winds increased to 40 to 60 mph, resulting in periods of blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As snow developed and began to accumulate during the evening on January 14, the increase in winds to a sustained 30 to 35 mph, with a few gusts as high as 41 mph at Cherokee (CKP), resulted in widespread near white-out conditions. Snowfall totaled 5.0 inches 4 miles north of Cleghorn and 3.0 inches at Cherokee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weaker winter storm moved into the area and added more ice and snow to the snow pack received the other day. This system moved in from the south and brought along a strong pull of warm air aloft. This led to a lot more ice accumulation than was expected prior to the event. While the storm was supposed to lead to mostly snow for the northern half of our forecast area, those areas received mainly freezing rain and sleet through most of the afternoon. This led to widespread ice accumulations of 0.10-0.25. In the southwest part of our forecast area, more ice accumulation was noted, with many locations reporting around 0.25 or even more. Along with the accumulating ice, some downed tree limbs and power outages were a result.||Mid afternoon, the freezing rain began to switch to snow from a line around Iowa City, IA to Freeport, IL. A relatively strong band formed in this location and dropped around 4 of snow, especially in the Iowa City area.  There were widespread reports of 2.3 inches of snow and sleet accumulation across eastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from 1.5 inches 3 miles west of Mount Sterling to 2.0 inches of snow and sleet in Selma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a single vehicle accident northwest of North Buena Vista (Clayton County) on January 16, 2021. Freezing drizzle coated the roads with a layer of ice causing the driver of a pickup truck to lose control on a curve, slide off the road and hit a rock bluff head-on. The driver was pronounced dead at the scene.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A northeast Iowa man was killed in a single vehicle accident during the evening of January 16th northwest of North Buena Vista. Light freezing drizzle had coated the roads with a layer of ice. The man lost control of his pickup truck on a curve, slid off the road and hit a rock bluff head-on. He was pronounced dead at the scene by authorities.

Forcing was particularly strong with the system, with lift within the dendritic growth zone exceeding -20 ubar/s for large portions of the state. Many areas saw snow rates exceed 1 inch per hour.

This led to a lot more ice accumulation than was expected prior to the event. While the storm was supposed to lead to mostly snow for the northern half of our forecast area, those areas received mainly freezing rain and sleet through most of the afternoon. This led to widespread ice accumulations of 0.10-0.25. In the southwest part of our forecast area, more ice accumulation was noted, with many locations reporting around 0.25 or even more. Along with the accumulating ice, some downed tree limbs and power outages were a result.||Mid afternoon, the freezing rain began to switch to snow from a line around Iowa City, IA to Freeport, IL. A relatively strong band formed in this location and dropped around 4 of snow, especially in the Iowa City area.  There were widespread reports of 2.3 inches of snow and sleet accumulation across eastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near New London reported 1.6 inches of snow and 0.1 inches of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved into the Midwest on the afternoon of January 30, 2021. Initially, precipitation began as rain as a layer of warm air was in place aloft. Over time, however, temperatures began to cool at the surface and aloft due to the falling rain, and precipitation changed to snow during the evening as a result. Some freezing rain was even measured as the transition occurred, and was most noticeable in the Cedar Rapids metro where photos showed accumulation on power lines.||Snow was widespread across the area during the morning of January 31, 2021, and all of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and far northeast Missouri saw some form of accumulating snow. Totals were highest in parts of east central Iowa and in north central Illinois, where locally heavy banding at times on the morning of January 31 produced higher snowfall rates. Overall, totals ranged from around 4 to 6 inches, with some locally higher amounts of 7 to 8 inches reported in Buchanan and Benton counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Columbus Junction reported 2.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the afternoon and evening of Wednesday, July 14.  Some of the storms produced damaging winds and numerous tornadoes.  Brief torrential rainfall was also observed with the stronger storm cells.  The areas that were hit hardest were in Buchanan, Delaware, Benton, Linn, and Jones counties in IA, where a total of 14 tornadoes occurred.  7 of the tornadoes had no visible damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado began in Tama County and crossed into Benton County, just south of Dysart, around 6:08 pm CST. As the tornado entered Benton County, the max width was around 560 yards. This includes the main tornado and satellite vortices, which are supported by video evidence. The tornado caused damage to trees and farm outbuildings at a farmstead just over the county line. This damage was rated EF-1, with maximum winds around 100 mph. The tornado then followed an intermittent path to the east-northeast for about 4.5 miles through rural areas, before dissipating.

Some of the storms produced damaging winds and numerous tornadoes.  Brief torrential rainfall was also observed with the stronger storm cells. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos from a trained spotter and high resolution satellite data confirm a brief tornado in a corn field southwest of Hopkinton. The tornado did not cause any damage that could be used to assign an EF-scale rating. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving arctic cold front brought dangerous conditions to portions of the area. Rain, mixed with freezing rain and sleet moved into eastern Iowa during the early morning, then as a cold front moved in from the west during the mid morning, the rain switched to snow. Strong winds and falling temperatures behind the front caused wet roads to quickly freeze.  Snow fell for several hours after the change over and became heavy at times, with widespread 2 to 6 inches of snowfall. Winds behind the front were 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 45 mph during the afternoon and evening causing blowing snow in open areas. Whiteout conditions were seen in open areas, and some areas experienced periods of near blizzard conditions, especially across east central and  northeast Iowa. Significant drifting snow continued into Feb. 5.  Wind chills dropped to 5 below to 15 below zero late Thursday night and Friday morning along and north of I-80.||Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, and road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain and wintry mix started the day, leading to snow (heavy at times), where between 2 and 5 inches of snow fell throughout Scott County. 4.6 inches of snow was reported at the local office. Strong winds were also noted in the afternoon, with a peak gust at 51 mph reported at the Davenport Airport ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel shortwave moved through the area producing storms with severe criteria hail and wind gusts. In Council Bluffs, 2 to 3 inches of rain fell over about an hour which led to a flash flood warning being issued. Flash flooding washed through basement apartments, swept up cars, and heaved pavement in low-lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported several flooded roads in the city. A couple of cars have been stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly moving storm system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and far northeast Missouri during the day on Wednesday April 7, 2021. The heavy rainfall occurred south of a Fairfield to Galesburg line with reports ranging from 1.13 inches in Burlington to 1.80 inches in Bushnell Illinois. A few of these thunderstorms became severe and produced damaging winds in McDonough, Henderson, and Hancock Counties during the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Damage was also reported to some mobile homes and a garage on the southwest side of Cedar Rapids due to a weak tornado that caused one minor injury due to glass from a broken window.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado caused damage to several manufactured homes and a garage in the southwest part of Cedar Rapids Wednesday evening. One home sustained significant roof damage, with additional siding damage to nearby homes. One person sustained minor injuries from glass after their bedroom window blew in. The tornado lofted debris into a nearby field. Maximum sustained winds were around 85 mph and the tornado was rated EF-0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Iowa was sandwiched between a northern plains surface low and mid atlantic high pressure, firmly in a southwest flow regime with tight pressure gradient lending support|for gusty winds most of the day. Model soundings were exceedingly dry with 40 degree dewpoint depressions and winds at the top of the mixed layer pushing 50 knots at times. This resulted in elevated to extreme fire weather concerns across the state and a red flag warning was issued. Deep mixing sent many locations into record warm temperatures that afternoon and winds gusted over 40 mph. A couple locations in northern and central Iowa gusted over 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air advection increased across the area ahead of a surface low moving |out of the southern Rockies. This developed an axis of 1000 j/kg MLCAPEs, but deep layer shear was marginal which limited much severe potential.  A few scattered thunder storms developed in the warm sector in afternoon followed by later development along a frontal boundary in the evening. Storms remain sub severe with the exception of a single report of half dollar sized hail associated with a stronger thunderstorm core late in evening in Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report via social media of half dollar sized hail. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An embedded shortwave within a longwave western US trough moved across the midwest, merging with a plume of increasing moisture transport out of the south. While no severe weather was reported, thunderstorms developed in the afternoon. Unfortunately one storm produced lightning that struck the Calhoun County court house and produced many thousands of dollars in damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported a lightning strike to the the county court house, resulting in over 20,000 dollars in damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the late afternoon of May 15th. One of these storms produced a brief tornado in rural Howard County and another dropped golf ball sized hail west of Fayette (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail fell west of Fayette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the late afternoon of May 15th. One of these storms produced a brief tornado in rural Howard County and another dropped golf ball sized hail west of Fayette (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media partners posted a public social media video loop taken outside of Riceville, IA that showed a brief touchdown that appeared like a landspout. No damage was reported and the video shows it was in an open field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following behind the rare December severe weather outbreak, very strong winds buffeted northeast Iowa from the late evening of December 15th into the morning of the 16th. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common with a few gusts over 60 mph. The highest measured wind gust was 63 mph at a mesonet station near Ionia (Chickasaw County). The high winds caused numerous power outages to occur across the area. The Governor of Iowa declared a state of disaster emergency for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Fayette, Floyd, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common across Mitchell County during the late evening of December 15th into the early morning of the 16th. A warming shelter was opened in Stacyville after the entire town lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracked across the Upper Midwest from December 10th into the 11th. The storm brought snow and strong winds to parts of northeast Iowa. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches were common across Mitchell and Howard Counties. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred as gusts of 30 to 40 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 11th. The highest reported snowfall total was 5.6 inches in St Ansgar (Mitchell County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 3 to 5 inches of snow fell across Mitchell County. Extensive drifting of the snow occurred during the early morning of the 11th as north winds gusted into the 30 to 40 mph range. The highest reported snowfall amount was 5.6 inches in St Ansgar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall led to flooding of fields and local roads in parts of Van Buren County overnight on the 27th. Van Buren and Lee Counties saw the worst of the rain, with accumulations around 2-3 inches, as two rounds of heavy rain moved through within 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in several secondary county roads closed overnight. Roughly at 190th Street east of Yellow Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface boundary across northern Iowa provided just enough focus for diurnally driven thunderstorm development. A number of thunderstorms along the boundary in northern Iowa produced pea to dime sized hail. Due to the very week flow, storms we slow moving and in some places pea sized hail began to accumulate on roads and turn filed white before melting. In Hancock County up to nickle sized hail was reported, the largest hail reported with storms that afternoon.  A brief landspout tornado also occurred in Calhoun County mid afternoon but did no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported dime to nickel sized hail lasting for twenty to twenty five minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief landspout tornado captured on video and posted in social media.  The tornado did not impact any damage indicators, hence the EF Unknown rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure dominated the eastern US with low pressure settling across the Pacific northwest. The resultant southerly flow allowed fro deep moisture transport from the Gulf into the Midwest. A subtle disturbance in the southerly flow moved into Iowa through the afternoon and evening hours with widespread shower activity. The moisture rich environment allowed for LCLs below 500 m, combined with 0-3 km CAPE near 100 J/kg and 0-1 km SRH near 200 m2/s2. Many of the showers exhibited rotation and a couple did produce low end tornadoes and wind damage. Interestingly, the deep warm clouds within this tropical air mass resulted in no lightning with any storm the entire night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of wind damage within the town of Winterset with sheds blown over near the county fairgrounds. A separate personal weather station in the area reported a wind gust of 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front helped lead to a severe weather outbreak in southwest Iowa where a microburst developed and is estimated to have produced 100 mph winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported significant damage near Persia, IA. 	A storm survey was conducted and noted 3 grain bins were destroyed and blown off of their foundations. Two buildings were completed destroyed. Peak wind gust was estimated at 100 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weak tornado occurred south of Woodward producing minor tree damage along its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought a mix of precipitation and gusty winds to eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri on Monday, March 15. Precipitation initially began as rain but changed to snow during the early morning hours of the 15th. The snow began to mix with sleet during the morning as cooler air moved into to the area aloft. Eventually, precipitation changed to all snow, and continued before winding down by midday and early afternoon. A light freezing drizzle continued along the Highway 20 corridor into the early evening hours.  Winds did gust to 50 MPH during the event making it hard to measure precipitation with drifting and blowing snow and sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official NWS observation at the Dubuque Regional Airport reported 3.8 inches of snow.  There was also a public report of 5.4 inches of snow 1 mile west northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough ejected off a western United States longwave trough, progressing across the northern Rockies. Lee cyclogenesis resulted in a surface low that moved across the central plains and towards northwest Iowa through the afternoon. A pronounced dry slot allowed for deep mixing and gusty winds to develop across portions of southwest Iowa through the afternoon. Sites across the area reported gusts of 50 to 60 mph and many had sustained winds between 35 to 40 mph for several hours. Increasing convergence across southern Iowa in the early evening hours aided thunderstorms development with a handful of hail reports ranging from penny to quarter sized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from a trained spotter and the public of quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed over northwest to west central Iowa late in the evening of June 16th, strengthening on the nose of warm air advection and the low level jet. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 60 mph winds accompanied by 10 minutes of hail from pea to dime sized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary draped across south central Iowa provided the focus for training severe thunderstorms on the evening of June 18th. With MUCAPE over 2000 J/kg and an inverted-v sounding profile, both large hail and damaging winds were supported. Storms developed along the front and produced hail from quarter to golf ball sized along with a report of up to four hail, plus damaging winds and heavy rain from training storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report via images on social media. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave dropped across Iowa, helping to increase warm air advection and moisture transport into the area. Steep lapse rates around 8 C/km and MLCAPE of 1000 J/kg was accompanied by deep layer shear of over 40 knots, creating an environment supportive of large hail. Scattered storms strengthened through the afternoon, with a few producing quarter to ping pong ball sized hail through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report via mping of ping pong ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave dropped across Iowa, helping to increase warm air advection and moisture transport into the area. Steep lapse rates around 8 C/km and MLCAPE of 1000 J/kg was accompanied by deep layer shear of over 40 knots, creating an environment supportive of large hail. Scattered storms strengthened through the afternoon, with a few producing quarter to ping pong ball sized hail through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep Gulf moisture fed into the state with the LLJ pushing a warm front north into southern Iowa through the late evening. Storms motion was generally parallel to the boundary, resulting in training of storms across southern Iowa and heavy rain. Many locations in southern Iowa received 3 to 6 inches of rain in a few hours with one location reporting over 7 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 4.17 inches of rain in 24 hours ending at midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm moved across the region on March 15th. This storm dumped sleet and snow on northeast Iowa with accumulations ranging from 3 to 9 inches. Roads became snow covered and slippery and numerous schools canceled classes for the day. The highest reported snowfall totals were 9 inches in Riceville (Mitchell County) and 8 inches in both Charles City (Floyd County) and around Bluffton (Winneshiek County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 8 inches of snow across Floyd County. Some sleet also occurred at the onset of the storm. The highest reported total was 8 inches in Charles City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving system brought late season snow to the area in the early morning hours of March 15th. Precipitation initially began as rain before transitioning over to snow across the northern half of the state. A band of heavier snow set up across the north allowing for snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hours at many locations. This was coupled with winds over 25 mph for several hours, significantly reducing visibility. By late morning the system had quickly exited the area, leaving behind widespread accumulations of 5 to 9 inches of snow across northern Iowa.

A frontal boundary draped across south central Iowa provided the focus for training severe thunderstorms on the evening of June 18th. With MUCAPE over 2000 J/kg and an inverted-v sounding profile, both large hail and damaging winds were supported. Storms developed along the front and produced hail from quarter to golf ball sized along with a report of up to four hail, plus damaging winds and heavy rain from training storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports lots of golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled out surface low across the area lead to a week of conditions favorable for funnel cloud development. In the afternoon and evening hours of June 25, 26, 27, 29, and 30, funnel clouds were reported across Iowa. 0-3 km CAPE values around 100 J/kg and surface vorticity in the vicinity of the surface low made for a good funnel cloud environment for several days, though there was no report of any tornadic activity with any showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a funnel cloud north of Granger, relayed via social media.

A fast moving system brought late season snow to the area in the early morning hours of March 15th. Precipitation initially began as rain before transitioning over to snow across the northern half of the state. A band of heavier snow set up across the north allowing for snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hours at many locations. This was coupled with winds over 25 mph for several hours, significantly reducing visibility. By late morning the system had quickly exited the area, leaving behind widespread accumulations of 5 to 9 inches of snow across northern Iowa.

A frontal boundary draped across south central Iowa provided the focus for training severe thunderstorms on the evening of June 18th. With MUCAPE over 2000 J/kg and an inverted-v sounding profile, both large hail and damaging winds were supported. Storms developed along the front and produced hail from quarter to golf ball sized along with a report of up to four hail, plus damaging winds and heavy rain from training storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 2.10 inches of rain in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave dropped across Iowa, helping to increase warm air advection and moisture transport into the area. Steep lapse rates around 8 C/km and MLCAPE of 1000 J/kg was accompanied by deep layer shear of over 40 knots, creating an environment supportive of large hail. Scattered storms strengthened through the afternoon, with a few producing quarter to ping pong ball sized hail through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported mostly pea to nickel sized hail with a few quarter sized stones mixed in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms moved across northern Iowa on June 11, resulting in a single severe report as a wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at the Estherville ASOS. The thunderstorm complex continued to weaken as it moved across the area through the rest of the morning as LLJ support was lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estherville ASOS recorded a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled out surface low across the area lead to a week of conditions favorable for funnel cloud development. In the afternoon and evening hours of June 25, 26, 27, 29, and 30, funnel clouds were reported across Iowa. 0-3 km CAPE values around 100 J/kg and surface vorticity in the vicinity of the surface low made for a good funnel cloud environment for several days, though there was no report of any tornadic activity with any showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a funnel cloud northwest of Altoona lasting 5 to 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early evening hours of March 23rd ahead of an approaching low pressure system. The overall severe threat was low and conditional based on whether clearing of cloud cover would occur and allow for generation of instability in conjunction with increasing warm air advection across southern Iowa. Thunderstorms developed along the warm front and displayed broad rotation, however no tornadoes occurred. The only damage occurred with a storm in Wapello county associated with brief strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Emergency Manager reported damage to out buildings and minor house damage along 160th Street north of Ottumwa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of an advancing cold front.  Storms produced some large hail up to two inches in diameter.  A tornado occurred just west of Pella producing EF1 damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down near Pella just to the northeast of the 195th Avenue and Highway 163 intersection.  The tornado tracked east-southeast for nearly 1.5 miles, dissipating just east of 210th avenue.  Tree damage and some structural damage to outbuildings was found, along with some shingle and siding damage to residences in the path.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter report of two inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved through Iowa and Illinois the evening of March 23, 2021.  As storms crossed a warm front near southeast Iowa, a brief tornado occurred in Lee County Iowa, north of Fort Madison. This tornado was rated EF-0, and destroyed a shed at a residence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado caused roof and tree damage and destroyed a shed at a residence north of Fort Madison. The tornado was rated EF-0 with maximum winds around 80 mph. There were no injuries. The Lee County Sheriff's Department and Emergency Manager assisted NWS Quad Cities with the storm survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms produced severe criteria wind in Pottawattamie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa mesonet site measured a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that moved from southern Minnesota during the early evening hours producing isolated damaging wind gusts and penny size hail as they moved into Buchanan County prior to midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer near Stanley reported dime to penny sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed behind a southward sagging frontal boundary.  With around 1000 J/kg MLCAPE and 40-45 knots of effective shear, a brief supercellular structure evolved. Even with hail size somewhat limited by available instability, substantial damage to total destruction of crops occurred due to the large quantity of wind-driven hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped off a one-foot diameter tree a few feet above the ground. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the afternoon and evening of Wednesday, July 14.  Some of the storms produced damaging winds and several tornadoes, most of which only briefly touched down.  Brief torrential rainfall was also observed with the stronger storm cells.  The areas that were hit hardest were in Delaware and Jones counties in IA, where a total of 5 tornadoes occurred.  An additional 7 tornadoes touched down in eastern IA, with no visible damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief touchdown in a field with no visible damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few supercell thunderstorms developed ahead of a warm front advancing into the forecast area, where they produced gusty winds, heavy rain, and hail up to 3 inches in diameter. Storms initially developed as more discrete supercells early in the evening, with the storm that went through Burlington being one of the more intense storms. Rainfall amounts reached 2 inches or more in some areas, as the anomalously moist environment favored excessive rainfall that lead to flash flooding. The La Moine River near Colmar also raised to Minor Flood stage due to the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported nickel size hail fell for 2 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled storm system centered near Galesburg Illinois brought several rounds of showers and embedded thunderstorms to eastern Iowa through the day. These showers and thunderstorms were slow moving westward across the area and were efficient rainfall producers.  Trained spotters reported 1 to 3 inches of rain in Van Buren County that led to flash flooding during the late morning and early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reports water flowing over a gravel road from nearby fields. When the water receded, piled up debris was left on the road .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moving southward across northern Iowa and southern Wisconsin and produced a damaging wind gusts as the thunderstorm's outflow moved into Dubuque. These winds knocked over two trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported 2 trees were blown down by thunderstorm outflow winds. The trees are about 2 feet in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe drought expanded into the western half of Clayton County in the middle of July. However, by the end of the month, only a small portion in the western part of the county remained in the severe drought after rain fell in the middle part of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought expanded into the western portions of Clayton County during the middle of July. Enough rain fell that by the end of the month only a small portion in the western part of the county remained in the severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that began in June across much of northeast Iowa continued through  the entire month of July for all or portions of Chickasaw, Fayette, Floyd, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that began in June across Fayette County continued for much of the county through July. The southern half of the county received enough rain in the middle of the month to be removed from the severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that began in June across much of northeast Iowa continued through  the entire month of July for all or portions of Chickasaw, Fayette, Floyd, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that began in June across Floyd County continued for almost all of the county through July. The northeast corner of the county received enough rain in the middle of the month to be removed from the severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that began in June across much of northeast Iowa continued through  the entire month of July for all or portions of Chickasaw, Fayette, Floyd, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that began in June across Howard County continued for a small part  of the county through July. Almost all of the county received enough rain in the middle of the month to be removed from the severe drought except for a small portion in the northwest part of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that began in June across Mitchell County continued for most of the county through July. The southeast corner of the county received enough rain in the middle of the month to be removed from the severe drought.

Almost all of the county received enough rain in the middle of the month to be removed from the severe drought except for a small portion in the southwest part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front helped lead to a severe weather outbreak in southwest Iowa where a microburst developed and is estimated to have produced 100 mph winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported multiple 7 inch tree branches down. Also noted dime sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of slow moving showers and storms developed over parts of northeast Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of July 1st. The activity formed a funnel cloud that was sighted near Saint Ansgar (Mitchell County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted near Saint Ansgar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported trees and powerlines down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported railcars blown over on Interstate 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small line of thunderstorms moved slowly northeast across parts of northeast Iowa during the early morning of July 14th. These storms briefly became strong enough to produce quarter sized hail in Maynard (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell in Maynard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two tornadoes skipped across portions of northeast Iowa during the late afternoon of July 14th. The first tornado impacted areas between Rockford (Floyd County) and Rudd (Floyd County) and to the east before dissipating northwest of Charles City (Floyd County). The most extensive damage occurred to a hog containment building near the end of its path. This tornado was rated an EF-1. The second tornado caused damage in Oelwein (Fayette County) and locations southwest of the city. This tornado ripped an air handler unit off a restaurant and caused damage at the high school football stadium. This tornado was rated an EF-0. The Governor of Iowa issued a disaster proclamation that included Fayette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 75 mph wind gust occurred in Floyd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado developed southwest of Oelwein and moved northeast into the city before dissipating. The tornado damaged the ticket booth, fencing and ripped metal siding off of stands at the high school football field. The tornado also blew out the sides of a greenhouse, tipped over the FFA's chicken coop and lifted but did not remove the roof on the main portion of the high school. At a restaurant, an air handler unit was ripped off and windows broken. Some crop and tree damage was noted southwest of the city as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that began in June across much of northeast Iowa continued through  the entire month of July for all or portions of Chickasaw, Fayette, Floyd, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that began in June across Chickasaw County continued for most of the county through July. The northern portion of the county received enough rain in the middle of the month to be removed from the severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave pushed across the area in the overnight to morning hours of July 9th, triggering severe storms over portions of western Iowa in the early morning hours. Storms weakened as they moved east into more stable air, however one cell did develop quarter to golf ball sized hail in Adair county.  As the early morning convection continued to weaken and move southeast, additional cells developed in late morning along outflow boundaries left across northern Iowa. By midday a single dominant cell strengthened over central Iowa and dove south southeast. This cell dropped golf ball to baseball sized hail northwest of and across the Des Moines metro through early afternoon. It then dissipated quickly just southeast of the Des Moines metro. The atmosphere recovered through the rest of the afternoon as surface based cape soared to 5000+ J/kg in southwest Iowa paired with 50 knots of 0-6 km shear.  Storms developed once more in the late afternoon across eastern Iowa along the outflow boundary put out by the midday supercell. Though the environment was supportive, no storms reached severe thresholds. Overnight yet another round of convection rolled across southern Iowa, producing heavy rain and pushing all lingering convection across eastern Iowa out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rain total of 3.00 inches.
